reasoning: The recent FRED data confirms the Federal Reserve is currently in an active easing (rate-cutting) cycle, lowering the federal funds rate upper limit target to 4.0% as of early November 2025. Policy changes generally only occur at scheduled FOMC meetings or via emergency cuts—rarely emergency hikes. With no meeting before November 30, a rate hike is effectively impossible, hitting the lower bound of 0.02. While there are typically meetings in mid-December 2025 and late January 2026, the Fed strongly prefers gradualism and forward guidance. Pausing a cutting cycle is possible, but abruptly pivoting to an unexpected rate hike by either December or February would require an unprecedented and sudden inflation shock. Therefore, the probability of a rate increase is exceptionally low across all three near-term resolution dates, with only minor probability increases appended to longer horizons to account for extreme macro tail risks.
